body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Reject oversized uploads before parsing multipart body**

`Upload` invokes `c.FormFile("file")` before any hard request-size limit is applied. `FormFile` triggers multipart parsing first, so an attacker can send very large payloads and consume memory/temp-disk resources before `SaveUploadedFile` enforces the 10MB check, creating a practical resource-exhaustion path on this endpoint. Apply a body-size cap (for example via `http.MaxBytesReader` and/or `MaxMultipartMemory`) before calling `FormFile`.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Reject oversized uploads before parsing multipart body**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Drop Content-Length when enabling gzip compression**

This middleware enables gzip for all matching requests but never removes or recalculates `Content-Length`. When downstream handlers set a fixed length (for example static/file responses), the header remains the uncompressed byte count while the body is compressed, which can produce `http: wrote more than the declared Content-Length` or truncated/invalid responses depending on payload size. Clear `Content-Length` (or recompute it) whenever compression is active.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Drop Content-Length when enabling gzip compression**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Pass UUIDs as quoted strings in action handlers**

The list renderer injects `item.id` directly into inline `onclick` handlers without quotes, but backend IDs are UUID strings (for example `07d7...-...`). This produces invalid JavaScript expressions or `ReferenceError` at click time, so edit/delete/view/download actions fail for real records in the UI. Wrap IDs as string literals (or use event listeners/data attributes) before invoking these functions.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Pass UUIDs as quoted strings in action handlers**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P0 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P0-red?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Escape user-controlled tags before injecting HTML**

This code renders `item.tags` directly into `innerHTML` without escaping each tag value. Because tags come from user input, an attacker can store markup/script payloads in tags and trigger stored XSS when any user opens the experience list, which is a security blocker. Encode tag text before interpolation or build DOM nodes via `textContent`.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P0 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P0-red?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Escape user-controlled tags before injecting HTML**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Load schema file without depending on process CWD**

Database initialization reads `internal/db/schema.sql` via a relative path, which only works when the server is launched from the repo root. In common deployments (systemd, containers, different working directories), startup will fail with `failed to read schema file`, making the service unavailable. Resolve the schema path from the executable location or embed the schema at build time.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Load schema file without depending on process CWD**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P0 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P0-red?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Escape filename before embedding in inline handler**

This interpolates `item.name` into a single-quoted JavaScript argument inside `onclick`, but `escapeHtml` does not make the value safe for JavaScript-string context. A filename containing `'` (or crafted payload like `a');...`) can break out of `downloadConfig(...)` and execute script when the list is rendered/clicked, creating stored XSS from user-controlled config names.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P0 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P0-red?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Escape filename before embedding in inline handler**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Delete physical file before removing DB record**

The handler deletes the metadata row first and only then attempts filesystem deletion; if `DeleteFile` fails (permission issue, transient IO, path problem), the API still returns success and the orphaned file is no longer referenced, causing an unrecoverable storage leak. Reverse the order or wrap both steps in compensating logic so failures do not silently leak files.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Delete physical file before removing DB record**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Increment download count only after successful file serve**

Download count is incremented before `c.File` attempts to read/send the file. If the file is missing or unreadable, clients get a failed download but the counter still increases, corrupting usage metrics. Move the increment after successful file delivery (or condition it on a successful open/send path).

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Increment download count only after successful file serve**

body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Quote Content-Disposition filename parameter**

The response sets `Content-Disposition` with an unquoted `filename=` value built from user-provided `cf.Name`. Names containing spaces or separator characters produce invalid/ambiguous header values, leading to inconsistent filenames across browsers during download. Emit a properly quoted/encoded filename parameter instead of raw concatenation.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
title: **<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Quote Content-Disposition filename parameter**
